Code fragment: public final TableField<AddressRecord, String> DISTRICT = createField(DSL.name("DISTRICT"), SQLDataType.VARCHAR(50).defaultValue(DSL.field("'N/A'", SQLDataType.VARCHAR)), this, "");
Explanation: The DISTRICT column uses 'N/A' as a default value to represent missing data instead of using NULL. This special marker value complicates queries and comparisons. Consider making the column nullable and using NULL to represent missing district information.

Code fragment: public final TableField<CategoryRecord, Long> CATEGORY_ID = createField(DSL.name("CATEGORY_ID"), SQLDataType.BIGINT.nullable(false).identity(true), this, "");
Explanation: The table has a synthetic auto-generated primary key (CATEGORY_ID) even though there's already a unique constraint on NAME (IDX_CATEGORY_NAME), which is a natural key suitable for identifying categories. Consider using NAME as the primary key since category names are inherently unique and immutable, eliminating the need for a surrogate key.

Code fragment: public final TableField<FilmRecord, MpaaRating> RATING = createField(DSL.name("RATING"), SQLDataType.VARCHAR.defaultValue(DSL.field("'G'", SQLDataType.VARCHAR)).asEnumDataType(org.tayrona.sakila.data.enums.MpaaRating.class), this, "MPAA Rating ENUM (G,PG,PG-13,R,NC-17)");
Explanation: The RATING column uses an ENUM type to restrict values to a fixed set (G, PG, PG-13, R, NC-17), which makes it difficult to add or modify ratings without schema changes. Consider creating a separate 'mpaa_rating' lookup table and referencing it via a foreign key.

Code fragment: public final TableField<FilmTextRecord, Long> FILM_ID = createField(DSL.name("FILM_ID"), SQLDataType.BIGINT.nullable(false), this, "");
Explanation: The FILM_ID column appears to reference the Film table but lacks a foreign key constraint to enforce referential integrity. Add a foreign key constraint referencing Film.FILM_ID (PK_FILM) to ensure data consistency.
